title: Mauby
subtitle: Caribbean mauby bark drink
excerpt: Mauby bark simmered with cinnamon, star anise, and cloves into a concentrated syrup, then diluted with cold water and sweetened to a bitter-sweet, deeply aromatic drink that is like nothing else. The initial bitterness gives way to a warm, spiced sweetness, the flavour is intensely distinctive and polarising. Mauby is the drink of the Eastern Caribbean, particularly beloved in Barbados and Trinidad.
sourceNotes: Mauby is made from the bark of the Colubrina elliptica tree, native to the Caribbean. The drink has been made in the Eastern Caribbean since pre-colonial times and is documented in Caribbean culinary literature from the 18th century. It is most associated with Barbados (where it is made by street vendors) and Trinidad, though versions appear across the Eastern Caribbean. The bitter-sweet flavour is an acquired taste that most Caribbean people associate strongly with childhood and home.

content.text: Mauby bark is available from Caribbean food shops. It looks like rough, dry chips of brown bark. The bitterness comes primarily from the tannins in the bark; the longer you simmer the concentrate, the more bitter it becomes. Taste the syrup before diluting. It should be intensely flavoured and pleasantly bitter.

The drink is diluted from concentrate at a ratio of roughly 1 part syrup to 5 parts water, though this varies by preference. Start with less syrup and adjust. The sweetness should balance the bitterness, too little sugar and the bitter note is harsh; too much and the character of the bark is lost.

Combine the {{mauby-bark}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, {{cloves}}, {{allspice}}, and {{ginger-root}} (if using) in a saucepan with 500 ml of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a medium simmer. Cook for 15 minutes. Strain through a fine sieve and discard the solids. While still hot, add the {{caster-sugar}} to the concentrate and stir until dissolved. Taste: the concentrate should be intensely bitter-sweet and heavily spiced. Allow to cool. To serve: combine 200 ml of concentrate with 800 ml of cold water (or adjust to taste). Serve chilled over ice.

items.fix: Use less bark (start at 20 g) and simmer for only 10 minutes. Dilute more heavily, try 1 part concentrate to 6 or 7 parts water
items.cause: Too much bark or too long a simmer

items.fix: Increase the bark to 40 g and simmer for the full 15 minutes. Taste the concentrate before diluting. It should be strongly flavoured
items.cause: Too little bark or too much dilution

items.fix: Add sugar after diluting rather than to the concentrate. This gives more control over the final sweetness
items.cause: Too much sugar in the concentrate

content.text: Mauby is the taste of the Eastern Caribbean in the way that sorrel drink is the taste of Jamaica at Christmas. In Barbados mauby vendors sell it cold from the street, pouring it from a large ice-filled bowl, and it is associated with hot days and physical thirst. In Trinidad it is sold by vendors at fetes and markets. The bitterness is the defining characteristic. It is not a drink you ease into the same way you would with a sweet fruit juice, and for many non-Caribbean people it is an acquired taste. For those raised with it, it is one of the tastes most strongly associated with home.
